Enterprise value (EV) is a company’s market capitalization plus debt, minority interest and preferred shares, and less total cash and cash equivalents.

OPEC stands for “organization of the petroleum exporting countries,” whose member countries seek to coordinate and unify their petroleum policies.

PE multiple is a financial metric that frames a company’s current stock price in terms of the company’s earnings per share.

A Basis Point is one hundredth of one percent.

As of March 31, 2025, the top ten holdings for the Longleaf Partners Fund: Albertsons, 6.6%; IAC, 6.4%; CNX Resources, 6.2%; Fidelity National Information Services, 6.0%; Mattel, 5.8%; FedEx, 5.5%; HF Sinclair, 5.2%; EXOR, 5.0%; Affiliated Managers Group, 4.7% and Bio-Rad, 4.6%. Fund holdings are subject to change and holdings discussions are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Current and future holdings are subject to risk.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Weak Earnings Forecasts Weigh On Market Outlook For 2025
data.description:
Earnings season kicks off this week, and it will be a very important earnings season, especially given the high levels of uncertainty surrounding the economy. The general trend for earnings has been that they have been coming down in recent months, and this has been one of the driving forces behind why the stock market has struggled in 2025.

If those estimates continue to struggle, they will continue to weigh on the market going forward, as they have already. If earnings guidance changes or is followed by further downgrades, it will have a pronounced impact on expected growth and, more importantly, the multiple investors are willing to pay for stocks.

Since September, earnings estimates have slipped to $264.21 per share from around $274.03 per share, a decline of about 4%, which accounts for some of the S&P 500 decline in 2025. Falling earnings estimates have sent the expected growth rate to just 11.2% from around 15%. Over time, sales estimates have also declined to $1,995 per share from $2008 per share, with growth slipping to 5.1% from 5.8%.

Additionally, we have seen the number of companies with negative earnings revisions rising and those with positive earnings revisions falling. With earnings mostly being revised lower and slower growth expected, it would make sense to see an index that has been struggling since the beginning of the year.

Margins Are Too High

The one aspect that seems not to have been negatively affected is margins. While they have fallen somewhat, they are still historically very high at 13.2%, which is more than 70 bps higher than 2024 and more than 30 bps higher than the previous recording in 2021, at 12.9%. This would seem to suggest that earnings are still at risk of falling further from their current levels. Historically, margins tend to start high and come down over time.

So while some of the recent market declines may have been tariff-related, it seems possible that the broader declines we saw before the tariff-related news were due to the underlying weakness in earnings forecasts. Assuming margins fall back to 2024 levels at 12.5%, it implies that 2025 earnings will fall to $249.50. That would be positive growth over 2024, but estimates would be about 5% lower than current levels.

2026 In Doubt

It also appears that estimates for 2026 would probably need to be adjusted lower, as those margins are currently sitting at more than 14%. Earnings are currently expected to rise to around $302 per share, but again, assuming margins that are closer to 2024-type levels. Then, earnings estimates for 2026 would need to decline to around $265, which is where the current 2025 earnings estimates are.

As we go through earnings season starting this week, some of these questions will begin to clear up and give a perfect sense of where the market is heading because markets generally trade with earnings, with sentiment, and growth rates the drivers of the multiples the market assigns.

So while the tariff war may be on pause for now, the underlying issues in the market may not.

data.title: Here Is A Solid Investment TIP
data.description:
At the beginning of February, I shared my positive perspective on Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities ("TIPS"), giving 15+ Year U.S. TIPS Index Exchange-Traded Fund ETF (LTPZ) a Strong Buy. Since that piece was published, LTPZ has dropped by 4% (with a return including dividends of -3.2%). I still like LTPZ a lot and own it in my IRA and in my trading account, but the trade idea has improved since then due to the tariff turmoil. I discussed the very large iShares TIPS Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:TIP) in that piece too, and it has been unchanged in price (+0.6% return with dividend). Today, I explain again what TIPS are, and I give TIP a Strong Buy as I discuss the risks and the opportunities.

The Current Tariff Turmoil

When Donald Trump was President the first time, he raised tariffs on China in response to Chinese theft of U.S. intellectual property. The two countries inched close to a trade war.

During the 2024 election, he discussed tariffs as a response to the drug trade and illegal immigration. After he won the Presidential election, he suggested tariffs imposed upon Canada and Mexico, as well as on Chinese goods.

In February, Trump shared a fact sheet on tariffs for these three countries. He ended up delaying implementation. In early April, the world was shocked by the actual proposed tariffs and stocks tanked. Trump delayed most of the tariffs again on 4/09 (for 90 days). A 10% tariff was maintained against almost all imports. The China situation has escalated.

Trump seems very set on tariffs, despite many of his supporters and most of his opponents fearing inflationary effects. The Federal Reserve Chairman, Jerome Powell, shared some comments that suggest that the Fed is in a tough spot.

Turning to monetary policy, we face a highly uncertain outlook with elevated risks of both higher unemployment and higher inflation. The new Administration is in the process of implementing substantial policy changes in four distinct areas: trade, immigration, fiscal policy, and regulation.

It's been a long, long time since the world was ramping up tariffs, and investors are highly concerned. Right now, it is a challenge for the U.S. government, which has a massive debt and must issue more debt (U.S. Treasury securities) as its existing debt matures.

Bear Market or Bull Market?

Gold has soared in 2025, and it has been strong for quite some time. In the piece I wrote on LTPZ, I shared a chart of SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) since its inception. Up now 23% in 2025 to a new all-time high, it has advanced a lot since that piece two months ago:

Gold is in a bull market! Perhaps gold, long viewed as a hedge against inflation, is benefitting from the pickup in inflation fears, or perhaps it is benefitting from a flight to quality. There are no countries in the world that have a "gold standard" any longer, though central banks do traffic in gold holdings. Gold has outpaced silver and other precious metals this year, as well as oil. Over the past decade, it has outpaced them, too.

Bonds were volatile last week, but are not that changed since year-end. Here are Treasury yields since the elections and year-end:

So, rates are generally lower since year-end, with the curve steeper. Since the elections, most Treasuries have increased in yield as the yield curve has steepened substantially. During this time, Fed Funds has been reduced by 0.75%. The 2-year Treasury and the 5-year Treasury yield less than Fed Funds, which is targeted at 4.25-4.50%.

Stocks seem like they are in a bear market. Most investors believe that a 20% decline in an index represents a bear market, and the S&P 500 traded below that level on Monday. The S&P 500 is currently down just 8.5% in price year-to-date. The Russell 2000 index is currently down 16.3% year-to-date, and it is 19.5% below its peak earlier this year. It traded more than 20% below its peak last week.

The iShares TIPS Bond ETF

In that last piece, I shared the history of TIPS, which was introduced in 1997. There is over $2 trillion outstanding, which is less than 10% of the outstanding U.S. federal debt. I also explained how TIPS protect against inflation.

Unlike other securities the Treasury issues, the TIPS principal is not fixed. The security was offered by the Treasury to appeal to investors concerned with inflation, as that can have a terrible impact on the prices of Treasury securities with long maturities. The interest-rate TIPS pays tends to be lower than regular Treasury yields, but the maturity amount is tied to inflation, through the Consumer Price Index from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. It provides a daily index ratio that helps investors to see how it is changing.

Many investors hold the TIPS themselves, but there are several large ETFs, including TIP, which currently has $14 billion in net assets. It holds 50 securities with maturities from 2026 to 2055 and an expense ratio of just 0.18%. It traded 2.9 million shares on Friday, which is a bit higher than its recent average daily trading and was about $313 million of trading value.

In 2025, TIP, with a total return of 2.0%, is exceeding cash returns to date slightly. Looking at it over the past five years along with two other ETFs, LTPZ and the iShares 0-5 Year TIPS Bond ETF (STIP), TIP has returned less than a money-market fund:

Comparing TIP with iShares U.S. Treasury Bond ETF (GOVT), a large fund run by the same company with an effective duration (5.67) that is similar to the TIP effective duration of 6.41, TIP has outpaced GOVT slightly year-to-date. Over the past decade, TIP has returned slightly more than cash, but it has far outpaced GOVT:

This return advantage is due mainly to the increased principal payments, which were boosted after the pandemic. Here is the price return of these two iShares ETFs:

TIPS and TIP ETF were a bit overheated in late 2021. Since the end of that year, TIP has declined slightly more in price than GOVT. Its total return, though, has been better at -5.6% versus -7.7%.

The TIP Chart

Over the last year, the price of TIP, which posted a 52-week high early in the month, has increased. There was a gap down in price on 4/7:

There is also a gap that remains open from January, down to 106.30. These gaps don't necessarily have to get filled, but the gap was very successfully tested on 4/11, when the ETF reversed in price to end slightly higher after gapping down and trading lower at first.

The returns of TIP will be highly dependent upon future CPI, as the interest payments are tied to that measure. To me, TIP seemed overdone earlier this month in the short term, but it could get back to $111 over time. In fact, I think that the price could do better. It traded to $131 in 2021, which was perhaps too high at the time. Here is the chart over the past few years:

I see several things in this long-term chart. First, there is a big gap above to about 114 that was created in September 2022. Second, the volume has picked up recently, highlighting potential investor interest. Third, the volume is still a lot lower than when the sell-off started in early 2022. Fourth, this looks like a reverse head-and shoulders formation.

Again, the total return ahead will depend on inflation. As far as thinking about the price over the next year, I think that it could advance by 15-20% to $124-130, which would leave it below the all-time high price from 2022.

This large, liquid monthly paying ETF states that its effective duration currently is 6.42 on its website, so this is effectively about a 3% decline in yield. Is that likely? Well, during the pandemic, the yields were slightly below 1% according to the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis. And, they were almost -1% in 2013. So, if inflation concerns do pick up, then the TIPS could drop in yield. TIP would rally tremendously. Those who accept this outlook should also consider buying LTPZ, which could advance more.

Risks with TIPS

TIPS have risks that I discussed in that piece in February and repeat here:

The biggest risk to TIPS is that inflation declines more than expected. Taking a lower yield to protect against inflation works out to be a wasted insurance policy. If inflation falls to 1% and the market reprices so that TIPS are offered at spreads of -1% relative to similar maturity Treasury securities, owners will miss out on big gains in price and get paid less at maturity. The current 10-year Treasury yield at 4.54% could drop to 2.50%, which would put its price up about 15%. The 10-year TIPS would increase by about 6%. The total return differential would be even greater, since the TIPS yield less than regular Treasuries. Of course, negative inflation would be even more harmful, as the principal value could decline.

Another risk to TIPS is that they are U.S. Treasury obligations, and they could get downgraded along with all Treasury securities. Investors in TIPS and regular Treasuries are taking debt from an entity that is highly regarded now but could face pressure due to the high debt.

This second risk seems bigger now than it did in February. TIP shares the same risks as TIPS, which it holds.

Trade Ideas

For those who like TIPS already and invest in them either directly or through an ETF or fund, extending durations makes sense in my view. The inflation-protection that the securities offer through higher yields if inflation kicks in is the same, but the longer maturities will perform better if investors begin to increase their interest. Here are the current yields according to Bloomberg:

Investors need to remember that the yields do not include future adjustments for inflation. On this basis, the 5-year TIPS yields 2.43% less than the 5-year Treasury. The 10-year yields 2.24% less, while the 30-year yields 2.19% less. Assuming 3% inflation, the yields for TIPS would be higher than Treasuries. If inflation ends up being higher, the returns would be even greater.

Over the last month, these yields have all increased more than the yields on the similar-maturity Treasuries. Over the past year, the 5-year TIPS yield has declined more than the 5-year Treasury, while the 10-year and 30-year TIPS yields have increased by more than the Treasuries.

So, the conclusion for those who want TIPS for their inflation protection is to extend maturities. For those invested in shorter funds or ETFs, this means buying TIP or even LTPZ.

I have been bearish on Treasuries for many reasons, and, for those who are concerned about inflation, it makes sense to sell Treasuries and buy TIPS in my view. Investors can exit Treasuries and buy TIPS or funds and buy TIPS funds, like TIP.

My final idea is, for those who hold gold due to inflation fears, TIP makes more sense. Gold, as measured by GLD ETF, has soared this year. Here is the long-term perspective of GLD and TIP:

I am actually short GLD through owning a leveraged inverse ETF, ProShares UltraShort Gold (GLL).

Conclusion

The financial markets are in turmoil, and I think the current situation bodes well for TIPS and the TIP ETF. I shared a price return for year-end of 15-20% higher, and the total return, which includes the interest payments and the potential principal adjustments, could be higher. Gold is soaring, the Federal Reserve has its hands tied, and inflation could reaccelerate.

Stocks are falling, but this bear market, the first since the one that ended in March 2009, may just be starting. Bond yields are up a bit for longer maturities since year-end, though down for shorter maturities.

Cash, which yields close to Fed Funds, seems attractive relative to Treasuries, and if inflation accelerates, it will perform better. But cash will likely underperform TIPS and the TIP ETF.

data.title: The Allstate Corporation: You Are In Good Hands
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I would say there isn't any doubt that The Allstate Corporation (ALL) truly lives up to its mantra, "You are in good hands." Since the financial markets started plummeting in early February, I have keenly been watching Allstate Corp.'s stock performance. One of the things that I noticed was that the stock was not affected as much as its peers, as shown below. It's like ALL had a pause moment and didn't react to the global panic, tariff news and the reciprocal tariffs imposed globally. Yet, before all this turmoil, the stock was doing pretty well. It had solid growth prospects, its earnings were looking promising, and it had quite a decent combined ratio.

As the announced tariffs that came into effect in April caused global financial markets to tumble, ALL stock fell to $180 per share. However, on 9th April, the White House reported that President Trump had issued a 90-day pause on tariffs and ALL immediately saw signs of a turnaround. It rose to $193 and it's one of the reasons why I definitely think the timing is great for The Allstate Corporation.

In my previous articles on Mercury, Skyward, Fidelis and IGIC where I analyzed specialty insurance companies, one of the key factors that I keenly look for is a profitable insurance business model and for sure ALL is no exception. In their recent earnings release, the property liability combined ratio increased from 91.2% in 2023 to 84.6%. This meant that for every $1.0 collected as premium, $0.84 was used for expenses and claims, leaving the company with $0.16 as profit before investment income. The protection homeowners segment had a combined ratio of 67.5% in 2023 and 62.5% in 2024. The CEO attributed this rise to an increase in premiums.

Property-Liability combined ratio was 86.9 for the quarter, which was 2.6 points better than the prior year, primarily due to higher average earned premiums and improved loss experience more than offsetting increased advertising investment and higher catastrophe losses.

In previous articles on specialty insurance companies, I have always had a bias towards the adjusted combined ratio and its importance in evaluating an insurer's profitability. This is because the ratio is the pillar of underwriting profitability. Given ALL has had a strong combined ratio across all their major businesses, I am confident in recommending ALL as a strong Buy.

The Catalyst

The Allstate Corporation announced its sale of its Health and Benefit business to Nationwide during its 4Q24 earnings release. This sale is expected to bring in $3.25Bn in proceeds and as a result, it will generate a financial book of $1.0 Bn in 2025. In this regard, I would expect the following:

  • The $1.0 Bn will increase the net income in 2025. This will be shown as a single item in the statement of financial performance. However, from a non-GAAP perspective, the $1.0 Bn might still inflate the revenues but won't count towards adjusted net earnings.
  • The transaction will increase the retained earnings and as a result, increase book value per share by $3.77 per share. This is under the assumption that shares outstanding remain at 265Mn.
  • By the end of 2025, the company will have a total of $21.9 Bn in available capital. This should give the company some dry powder to pursue other activities such as the shares repurchase program and other investments as noted by the CEO.

Growth

SA Quant ratings have The Allstate Corporation growth prospects at A+ growth grade. This growth is as a result of its organic and sustainable growth of 12.28% which is above the sector median of 5.91%. Additionally, in the last 5 years, ALL's average growth rate has been 9.19% which, I believe, is sustainable and consistent.

Profitability

In terms of profitability, SA Quant ratings have ALL's profitability grade at A+ profitability grade. As we previously observed, its combined ratio is within profitability levels. With most of their business segments operating under 85%, it's clear that ALL strives to run at profitability despite experiencing a couple of catastrophic losses in 2024. Additionally, the sale of its Health and Benefits business is a testament to moving the core business from struggling areas to profitable business lines while maximizing shareholder value, as mentioned by the CEO:

The decision to maximize shareholder value by combining the Health and Benefits businesses with companies that have strategic alignment also was successful with two of the three businesses now under contract to be sold for $3.25 billion

Valuation

In regard to valuation, ALL trades at a forward EV/Sales of 0.88x, which represents a 69.82% discount against the sector median. Additionally, ALL is trading at a forward price/sales of 0.74x, which is a 71.13% discount to its sector median.

In comparison with its peers, ALL's EV/sales stand at 0.95x, while American International Group (AIG) trades at 2.13x, The Travelers (TRV) trades at 1.38x, Tokio Marine Holdings (OTCPK:TKOMY) trades at 1.11x, and Intact Financial Corp (OTCPK:IFCZF) and Arch Capital Group (ACGL) trade at 1.96x and 2.12x, respectively.

Allstate's Key Risk

One of the biggest risks I see with ALL is that the company may not be in a position to complete the sale of its Health & Benefit business segment. This will definitely hurt their projected financials in 2025, which could, in turn, affect their multi-year transformative strategy. Failure to dispose of these assets will still have an exposure in the financial book, as the operating results will continue to be reported in ALL's books as part of net income until completely sold off. Until all standard closing conditions are met, including regulatory approvals, the health business will still be under Allstate and will continue weighing in on its balance sheet.

There is also another risk that ALL could experience large catastrophic events. In 2024, ALL reported catastrophic losses worth $4.96 Bn, though the company has strict risk management controls and loss management experience. These CAT losses were attributed to LA wildfires, hurricanes, and severe storms. However, with ALL's top-notch risk and return management, the company seems to have well-defined risk modeling, as noted by the CEO:

Homeowners insurance generated $1.3 billion of underwriting income for the year while covering $3.7 billion of customers' catastrophe losses, reflecting an industry-leading business model. We rapidly supported customers impacted by the January California wildfires and related losses are expected to be about $1.1 billion, pre-tax, net of reinsurance, reflecting a decision to reduce market share beginning in 2007 and a comprehensive reinsurance program.

Final Thoughts

As mentioned earlier, what stood out most with ALL, was its performance between February and April 2025. As markets and stocks were plummeting due to panic, tariffs, and fears of a recession, ALL stood strong. While this tends to be the case with large insurance companies, I believe there is something special about Allstate. And as we have seen, ALL is shifting from non-performing business to profitable business lines. The adjusted combined ratios for their property-liability business improved to 84.6%, protection auto improved to 93.4% from 99.9%, and protection homeowners improved from 67.3% to 62.5%. This clearly explains its profitability grade, growth prospects, and the valuation discount. In any insurance company, the largest risk is catastrophic losses, and as we have observed, ALL is in control of its risk management framework, even with the 2024 $4.96 Bn catastrophic payout. This is a strong company with consistent profits, reliable growth prospects, and an intentional effort to generate shareholder value.

data.title: SCHY: A Solid 4%-Yielding Non-U.S. Dividend ETF
data.description:
The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF (SCHY) is a potentially attractive investment option for investors that want to build equity exposure outside of the U.S. The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF has a much shorter performance history than the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and also manages significantly less capital.

However, the ETF could be an attractive dividend option for investors that want to diversify into the best dividend stocks outside of the U.S. market: the SCHY specifically screens for non-U.S. dividend stocks that have paid a dividend for at least 10 years, ensuring that only the most predictable dividend-paying companies are included in the ETF's portfolio.

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF also outperformed Schwab's larger U.S.-focused ETF in terms of dividend growth and has a higher initial yield offer. With a competitive expense ratio, I believe the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF could grow into a much larger ETF going forward.

Previous rating

While I didn’t cover the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF before, I have had a strong buy rating on the U.S. version of Schwab’s dividend ETF: Why I Am Aggressively Buying The Drop. The main reason I liked the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF is that the ETF has delivered double-digit annual NAV returns since its inception and has grown its dividend for a long time as well.

Because of this, the SCHD has been a solid compounding machine for dividend investors, although its dividend growth has slowed in recent years. Investors that want, for diversification reasons, to add an ETF focused on large-cap, high-yielding non-U.S. stocks to their portfolios may want to take a look at the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF.

Strong diversification profile, portfolio split

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF pales in comparison to its bigger cousin, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF, in terms of size: the SCHY manages $844M in net assets compared to $66.6B for the SCHD. Both ETFs offer investors the opportunity to invest in dividend stocks from companies that have focused on growing their dividends with the major difference being the geographic orientation of the fund. Both ETFs also offer comparable expense ratios which helps investors compound their invested capital quicker: the SCHY has an expense ratio of 0.08% compared to 0.06% for SCHD.

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF is focused on large-cap multinationals and has exposure to Europe, Australia, Asia and Latin America. The three biggest countries by investment destination were the United Kingdom (15%), Australia (12%) and France (11%). The Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF, on the other hand, only owns U.S. stocks.

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF owns a diversified portfolio of dividend-paying stocks whose names include leading non-U.S. consumer, energy and pharmaceutical companies. The biggest holding for the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF is ENEL, an Italy-based manufacturer and distributor of energy and gas.

This company currently has a portfolio weighting of 4.42% which is followed by Ono Pharmaceutical with a weighting of 4.40% and Unilever with a representation of 4.21%. As you can see in the portfolio split below, the top ten holdings are of fairly equal size, making the SCHY a highly attractive investment option for investors seeking diversification (both in geographic terms, as well as, in terms of portfolio size).

In my opinion, the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF can be expected to do well, in terms of both net asset value and dividend growth, as long as the European economy is growing. The top three industries, with an about equal ~15% weighting are Financials, Consumer Staples and Communication Services.

NAV returns compared: SCHD vs. SCHY

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF was launched on April 29, 2021, which means the ETF has a relatively short investment history. For comparison, The Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF was launched on October 20, 2011 and has therefore a much more time-tested (and longer) performance track record. The SCHY delivered an annual NAV return of 3.97% since its inception about four years ago, while the SCHD is looking much better, mainly due to the outperformance of the U.S. stock market in the last decade: the SCHD returned 12.93% annually since 2011.

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF under-performed its larger ETF cousin over its relatively short period of existence. In the longer term, the Schwab U.S. Dividend ETF has delivered very strong NAV results and has proven itself as a very capable wealth-building vehicle. Since its inception in 2011, the SCHD has delivered a total NAV return of 200% which makes the ETF an attractive long-term investment option or investors that want to bet on the U.S. market. For investors that want international exposure, the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF could be an attractive investment vehicle, especially given the ETF's strong screening criteria, promising NAV profile and low expense ratio).

SCHY offers a slightly better yield

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF is currently trading at an initial dividend yield of 4.38% which compares against a yield of 4.16% for the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF.

Risks with SCHY

The biggest risk for the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF is that the ETF may not grow its net asset value as fast as the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF and therefore lose out with investors. The SCHD has delivered double-digit NAV returns since it was launched in 2011. If the SCHY fails to achieve higher net asset value returns going forward, the ETF may lose fund assets.

Final thoughts

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF could be an interesting addition to a dividend investor’s portfolio, especially if said investor is looking for diversification outside of the U.S. market. The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF owns some of the biggest European energy, pharmaceutical and consumer stocks, all of which have a history of growing their dividends. While the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF does not have as long a record as its larger U.S. cousin, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF, the ETF has a ton of potential to grow its dividends long term. Additionally, the yield looks good (4%+) and the expense ratio is low. In my opinion, dividend-focused retirees and conservative investors may find value in the ETF's different geographic positioning that provides a hedge against a major downturn in the U.S. market.

data.title: Snowflake: It Is Time To Buy Tech Again (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
It is time to warm back up to Snowflake (SNOW). The company appears to be at an inflection in growth rates, with management confident that net retention rates may be stabilizing. Importantly, the valuation is such that one does not need an acceleration in top-line growth for the story to work out. While SNOW admittedly trades at a relative premium to peers, I expect this premium to persist given the attractive secular story. As the company executes against expanding profit margins, I expect the stock to deliver strong forward returns. I am upgrading the stock to a buy rating.

SNOW Stock Price

I last covered SNOW in January, when I downgraded the stock due to both valuation, as well, as my deteriorating view of the growth story. The stock has declined around 11% since then.

There are cheaper names to be found in software as the entire sector has re-rated downward, but SNOW nonetheless looks attractive here.

SNOW Stock Key Metrics

SNOW is an enterprise tech company best known as a data warehouse, helping its customers to organize and get the most value from their data. The company was a very popular stock ever since it came public based on the “growth of data” story and that became even more relevant following the rise of generative AI, but the company has failed to show a sustained acceleration in revenue growth rates. I suspect that many investors may be now losing faith that generative AI may be the tailwinds that they had been hoping for.

In the most recent quarter, SNOW delivered 28% YoY product revenue growth to $943 million, demolishing guidance of between $906 million and $911 million. I note that management typically aims for an accurate guidance target, making that outperformance quite notable.

The company saw current remaining performance obligations grow 27% in the quarter, representing a strong result but decelerating sequentially from the 36% number posted in the third quarter. I have previously viewed cRPO growth as being a leading indicator for revenue growth, but am no longer of this view given what I have seen in the sector over the past few years.

SNOW saw its net revenue retention rate decelerate by a hair to 126%. On the conference call, management noted that they do not believe that it will drop to 124% or 125% but should stabilize “in the mid-120s.”

On a slightly sour note, SNOW is one of the few software names in my coverage universe that has not been making consistent progress on profitability. The company’s GAAP operating loss has somehow expanded this year. It is possible that management is waiting for revenue growth rates to decelerate more meaningfully before turning the gears on profitability.

SNOW ended the quarter with $5.3 billion of cash and investments versus $2.3 billion of debt, representing a solid net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management has guided for the first quarter to see up to 22% YoY revenue growth to between $955 million and $960 million with 5% non-GAAP operating margins. Management has guided the full year to see around 24% product revenue growth.

Management outlined expectations for the second half to “show more muster” due to new products. Management commented that they are “now seeing Iceberg as more of a tailwind” as it is “opening up so much more data for us.” Investors (including yours truly) had previously been worried that the proliferation of Iceberg tables might be a material headwind, but management noted that they have “yet to see massive amounts of data move out of Snowflake.”

Is SNOW Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, SNOW traded hands at just around 11x forward sales.

I should first note that many software names have sold off in this quarter, and those looking for cheaper value might be advised to look elsewhere. Yet, SNOW has a compelling thesis here. The company’s exposure to the growth of data will arguably be an attractive story for years to come. While the market uncertainty created by tariffs might lead to reduced demand for IT spending over the next year, I do not see any material impact on the long-term thesis. I can see this name sustaining at least a 10x sales multiple, if not higher. The company might reach 30% net margins over the long term - 10x sales equates to around 33x earnings. With the company projected to sustain 18% to 22% revenue growth over the next many years, the stock might deliver annual returns of around 16% to 18% after accounting for ongoing operating losses and mild multiple compression. That looks like an attractive return proposition from a leader in enterprise tech.

SNOW Stock Risks

It is possible that demand for enterprise tech and SNOW declines due to macro pressures. It is possible that generative AI will have a disruptive effect on the business. I have noted that the stock is not relative to peers. This, plus the company’s lack of strong profit margins, may make the stock more exposed to volatility in the event of a market downturn (with the tariff war in full swing, this may be an underappreciated risk). I note that it is not entirely clear if the company has superior products than competitors like Databricks.

SNOW Stock Conclusion

It is finally time to be buying tech again, and SNOW looks attractive here after the selloff. The company has a net cash balance sheet and continues to sustain aggressive top-line growth rates. I see a clear path to market-beating returns from here, though we may see some volatility in the near term. SNOW shares many of the characteristics that I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am upgrading SNOW to a buy rating.
